aliases: Filipino Chicken Rice with Sambal Kangkong

english: Filipino Chicken Rice with Sambal Kangkong

food_subgroup: Poultry-based rice dish with stir-fried spicy vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sambal kangkong chicken rice specifically. FOODON_00002572 (chicken rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for sambal kangkong chicken rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of sambal kangkong chicken rice typically comprises approximately 200 g steamed white rice, 120 g poached or roasted chicken, and 130 g stir-fried sambal kangkong, totalling approximately 450 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component foods: Hainanese Chicken Rice (HPB Singapore Foodo), Stir-fried Kangkong with Sambal (HPB Singapore Foodo), and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy steamed white rice
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Database; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal kangkong chicken rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: approximately 44% steamed white rice (HPB/USDA), 27% poached chicken (HPB Foodo chicken rice entry), and 29% stir-fried sambal kangkong (HPB Foodo). Sodium is elevated due to sambal paste and soy sauce. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite estimate consistent with HPB Foodo data for analogous Singaporean chicken rice dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice dishes; analogous estimate based on steamed white rice GI (Atkinson et al., 2008, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal kangkong chicken rice. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white rice (GI ~64–72, mean ~68 for jasmine/long-grain) moderated downward to 65 to account for the protein and fat content of chicken and the fibre contribution of kangkong, consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation principles (Atkinson et al., 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 57.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 19.5 × 4.5 = 87.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sambal paste, shrimp paste (belacan), and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, representing 60–72% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and sambal, or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 57 per full serving is high for individuals with type 2 diabetes; portion control of the rice component, substitution with brown rice where available, and pairing with the protein-rich chicken and fibre-containing kangkong can help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium and saturated fat from chicken skin and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 5 g per serving) and elevated sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken, request less oil in the sambal preparation, and limit frequency of consumption to reduce cumulative cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Kangkong Chicken Rice is a composite Southeast Asian dish served at Filipino-operated casual dining establishments in Singapore, combining three core components: steamed white jasmine rice, poached or roasted chicken, and stir-fried kangkong (water spinach, Ipomoea aquatica) cooked in sambal chilli paste. The dish reflects the culinary overlap between Filipino and Singaporean food cultures, where sambal — a pounded chilli-based condiment made with dried chillies, shallots, garlic, and shrimp paste (belacan) — is applied to the leafy green kangkong, a staple vegetable across Southeast Asia. The chicken component is typically prepared in the Hainanese style (poached in seasoned broth) or roasted, and served sliced over fragrant steamed rice. The sambal kangkong provides a spicy, umami-rich, slightly smoky flavour profile with a tender-crisp texture, contrasting with the soft rice and succulent chicken. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 666 kcal per 450 g serving, with meaningful protein from chicken (approximately 38 g per serving), complex carbohydrates from rice, and micronutrients including vitamin C and iron from the kangkong. Sodium content is elevated due to sambal paste and soy sauce, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is high per full serving due to the white rice base, which is a consideration for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal kangkong chicken rice as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging using HPB Singapore Foodo data for analogous chicken rice and stir-fried kangkong dishes, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for steamed white rice. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice, moderated for mixed-meal composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or an HPB Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy component sources for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo component sources. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation of this dish per HPB Foodo analogous entries. 6. The cuisine tag in the input JSON lists 'Filipino' as primary, which is consistent with Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road, a known Filipino casual dining establishment in Singapore. The dish is a Filipino-Singaporean fusion item. 7. Halal status is marked true as the standard preparation uses chicken and sambal without pork; however, individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 8. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for chicken and cooking oil; these are approximate. 9. GI set to 65 (medium) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database steamed white rice data, moderated for mixed-meal protein and fibre attenuation.
